# Transcrição: 04-The Ads Sniper Method

- **Fonte:** 04-The Ads Sniper Method
- **Duração:** 11:02
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:01]

The addSniper method is something which I would recommend that you do every week at least, if not every day, especially at the higher spend levels. So with something like this, where I'm spending about, you know, $500, $1000 a week, that kind of level, I'm not going to check in too frequently on the kind of day-to-day tweaking and optimizations because the more optimizations you make, the more you destabilize performance. So there is a balance between over-optimizing and trying to get stability. So the more stable your ad account becomes, the less frequently you'll have to tweak and optimize, but also the less you tweak and optimize, the more stable your ad account becomes. And getting that balance right is so important. So here in this example, we have a few different ad sets that are running in this example campaign. And we can see that week on week, we're spending about $1000 a week, give or take. And within the Romass target we want above 2, ideally, it's kind of doing okay. So at this level, you'd say that, you know, the performance is good. I'm not too worried about optimizing and things like that. But as I keep saying throughout this, the devil is always in the detail. So for example, if I break this ad set down and I look at the ad set performance, now this is different story. So although average of 2.08 ROAS is okay, this ad set here is giving me 2.5. This one's giving me 2.25. Part of the ad sniper strategy is to pull out poor performance to optimize for better performance. So here, for example, I'm seeing consistently lower ROAS than these campaigns here. So my action here would be to actually pause this ad set out. Now with CBO, Facebook is trying to balance the spend across those different ad sets and the ads within to give you the best performance overall. But it does also often need human interaction to give feedback as to what's acceptable or not. Here's the thing. The campaign I'm running right now, it's profitable, but it's on auto bid. So Facebook doesn't really know what a good cost per acquisition is, what a good return on ad spend is, and therefore I need to have a bit more control over this. If I go into manual bid, then Facebook has more of an idea of what I'm aiming for. And that's something we cover in the scaling section. But at least for now, I need to intervene and step in to improve that performance. So here, for example, the most obvious thing would be to pause this ad set because overall it's not been performing. But what I want to do is to see if any particular ads are actually performing okay and whether there's potential or not.

## [03:01 - 06:08]

And here I can see, for example, this ad here was kind of okay. It started off at 2.28. 1.7 is close, not quite. Overall, there isn't a great deal amount of good performance for me to identify that this has potential. So this has been through our graduation testing process. At 1.6 ROAS, it's still profitable, but it's not as profitable as these adsets. And what I would do in this instance is I would pause this ad set. I would move it into a separate campaign and try and duplicate it and maybe go through grad testing and pull out new offers and promotions and see if I can find new angles and things like that. Because at 1.6 ROAS, it's still profitable, but I know I can do more. So I need to do more work to tweak and optimize this and give these two audiences here a better chance of success and scaling and getting the budget that it needs so that at the campaign level as we continue increasing our budget, just by pausing this before we increase our budgets, our ROAS will instantly increase. And the next step is I'll give that 24 hours to settle, maybe even 48 hours. Then I'd go through my scaling process to start pushing the budget up because now I've got a good base level ROAS, so I want to make sure I can maintain it. The other thing about ad sniping is it's not just looking at the high level of your ad sets and ads. It's also looking at your breakdowns. It's so important to have a look at where your performances are perhaps better than others. So for example, if I take this ad set here, which hasn't been performing well overall, and I look at this and say 1.61 is not as good as 2.5, etc., how about the breakdown of data within that? Now, the 35 to 44 audience here has actually showed some promise. Let's just get rid of our time breakdown and let's just look at age alone. So we look at the age bands for this one ad set, which is we are looking to cut out. And over that period, although we've got a 1.61 overall, the 55 to 64 audience is showing a lot of potential. And actually the audience that's actually bringing down the performance is the 25 to 34 and also the 45 to 54. So now the whole process of ad sniper is to really laser pinpoint where the opportunities are and cut out the bad opportunities. So my next step here is still to pause this ad set. But what I will likely do is to take it into its own campaign and start playing around with the breakdown. So this is a mal 35 to 65 campaign. And for what the reason this age band here, in fact, I would argue the 55 plus age band is working far better than anything below. So I would probably split that ad set into two 50, 54 and below and 55 and above and

## [06:08 - 09:12]

test that. But we can go further. We can have a look at our placements and see if there's anything else going there. So let's have a look at platform first. And we look at our split between Instagram and Facebook. Actually, surprisingly, for this audience, Instagram is actually performing much better. So I look at this and say there's a fairly even performance across both. My next test could be by splitting by age, but also focusing on placement optimization as well, which means serving the ads that are best placed for that platform. For example, here again, you can see Instagram is performing better than Facebook. Whereas here, Facebook is performing better than Instagram because these are audience specific details that are coming out from my activity to find profitable audiences. And for me, I'm looking at Raya Sear for other accounts. You might have a look at Cosper Lead or Cosper Registration, et cetera. But the goal is to keep drilling down into where your performance is. So now if I go into exact placements and I go back to my candidate ad set that we're looking with and originally looking to pull out and see if there's anything here that stands out in particular, I would argue there isn't so much at the moment. I could even go and look at my breakdown of region. So this is a US campaign. Maybe there's something by state level where there's particular spend and performance. But this is like Florida. Maybe there's a split here that looks specifically at Florida and the 55 plus market. Illinois here as well, which has come out. I'm a fan of big audiences and less kind of breakdowns of different particular niches and things like that where you can because you want to give more and more data to Facebook to do its optimization. So I know that Florida has a fairly big retirement community. Maybe there's something that and, you know, it does obviously require some kind of level of local knowledge, but at least looking at the data, I can start asking questions why are certain states performing better? ## [09:12 - 11:02]

and I need to adapt my targeting. So this is the kind of breakdown that you get from the ad sniper strategy, which is just like a laser target site in a gun, for example, you're looking to shoot down bad performance and pull out good performance. So on one hand, you're mining for the gold. On the other hand, you're shooting down the bad segments, the bad options, etc. So it's really important to do this analysis once a week. If you're really spending above, let's say 10 K a day, you might want to do it a few times a week. But certainly at the very minimum, this is an activity that you want to go through on your key ad sets and at the ad level, potentially as well, to find those pockets of opportunity to see where potentially you're wasting spend and where you can optimize spend. So for example, you know, we look at this ad set overall at 1.61. Already we've identified an age band, potential placement optimization, and maybe even maybe even state based optimization. This ad set could turn into a really successful campaign. However, if we just looked at this at the top level and pulls this out and moved on, we could have missed huge opportunities for profit and scale. So this is a big part of the ad sniper strategy is to keep looking at your segments and your data and find those opportunities and find new ways to target and segment. This is not always a case of going back to phase one and phase two of grad testing and finding new audiences, finding new ads. Sometimes those opportunities are in existing data. And like I say, the devil is behind the data. There is good opportunity here. You just need to find it and optimize for it.
